food_name: Kuih Lapis (Steamed Multi-Layered Tapioca and Coconut Milk Kuih)
aliases: steamed tapioca coconut milk layered cake

english: steamed tapioca coconut milk layered cake

cuisine: Singapore Malay / Malaysian Malay / Indonesian / Peranakan
region_of_origin: Malay Archipelago — Malaysia, Singapore, and Indonesia

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Kuih Lapis. Closest parent is steamed tapioca starch cake with coconut milk. No exact FoodOn URI can be assigned without fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Steamed multi-layered tapioca starch and coconut milk kuih (Malay kuih lapis style)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CEK used as closest parent for steamed starch-based confection; no exact Kuih Lapis entry exists in FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Kuih Lapis or equivalent steamed tapioca-coconut milk layered kuih.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Kuih Lapis is 3 pieces (approximately 90g), as commonly sold at hawker stalls and kuih trays in Singapore and Malaysia.

nutrient_source.title: Kuih Lapis — Health Promotion Board (HPB) Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database; cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore; Institute for Medical Research (IMR), Malaysia
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Kuih Lapis is listed directly in the HPB Singapore Foodo database. Values cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD for consistency. Both are approved primary regional sources under Project Veritas v2 source policy.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Tapioca Starch and Rice Flour-Based Foods — University of Sydney GI Database; tapioca starch GI ~70–95, rice flour moderate-high GI
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Kuih Lapis exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 70 is estimated from the primary starch components: tapioca starch (GI ~70–95) and rice flour (GI ~70–80). The lower bound of 70 is used as a conservative estimate. Glycemic load per 90g serving = (70 × 28.5 × 0.9) / 100 = 17.9, rounded to 18.0. Assessment type: analogous estimate from approved GI source.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (85 mg per 100g; 77 mg per 90g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Kuih Lapis is low in sodium and does not pose a meaningful sodium burden for hypertensive patients; however, its high saturated fat content (7.5g/100g) warrants moderation as part of an overall cardiovascular-protective diet.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI 70), high sugar (12.5g/100g), and high glycemic load per serving (GL ~18)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should limit Kuih Lapis to one small piece (approximately 30g) as an occasional treat, as the combination of high-GI tapioca starch, refined rice flour, and added sugar produces a significant postprandial glycaemic spike; a standard 90g serving delivers a glycemic load of approximately 18, which is classified as high.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very high saturated fat (7.5g/100g, predominantly from coconut milk), representing ~38% of daily saturated fat limit per 90g serving
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should consume Kuih Lapis sparingly, as a 90g serving delivers 6.75g of saturated fat — approximately 34–38% of the recommended daily saturated fat limit of 20g — which may adversely affect LDL cholesterol levels when consumed regularly.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High saturated fat and high sugar — a proinflammatory dietary combination
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should limit Kuih Lapis to one small piece on festive occasions, as the combination of high saturated fat (7.5g/100g from coconut milk) and high added sugar (12.5g/100g) promotes systemic inflammation and may exacerbate disease activity.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kuih Lapis (also known as Kueh Lapis or Kue Lapis Sagu) is a traditional Malay and Peranakan steamed layered kuih iconic across Malaysia, Singapore, and Indonesia. It is visually distinctive for its alternating brightly coloured layers — classically pink or red alternating with white, or green alternating with white — created by steaming individual layers of batter one at a time in a square or rectangular tin. Each layer is poured and steamed until just set before the next is added, a labour-intensive process that produces the signature ribbon-like horizontal bands. The batter is made from tapioca starch, rice flour, coconut milk, sugar, and food colouring (traditionally pandan extract for green, or synthetic red/pink colouring). The resulting texture is silky, slightly sticky, bouncy, and chewy, with each layer peeling apart cleanly — a hallmark of a well-made Kuih Lapis. It is a quintessential tea-time snack, a staple of the Malay kuih tray, and a festive treat during Hari Raya and other celebrations. Nutritionally, Kuih Lapis is energy-dense at 188 kcal per 100g, with a high saturated fat content of 7.5g/100g derived almost entirely from coconut milk, and a high sugar content of 12.5g/100g from added sugar. It has a high glycemic index (estimated GI ~70) due to its refined tapioca starch and rice flour base, making it unsuitable for regular consumption by individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular disease. It is gluten-free, halal, and dairy-free, and is suitable for vegetarians. A standard serving is 3 pieces (approximately 90g), delivering approximately 169 kcal, 6.75g saturated fat, and a glycemic load of ~18.

confidence_reason: Kuih Lapis is listed directly in the HPB Singapore Foodo database, an approved primary regional source. Values were cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD. Core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are fully populated from verified sources. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using the primary starch components (tapioca starch and rice flour), which is the best available approved evidence for this traditional preparation.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Kuih Lapis is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or IMR Malaysia MyFCD as of April 2026; the fat is predominantly saturated (coconut milk). water_g set to -1: moisture content for this steamed kuih preparation is not reported in the HPB or IMR sources consulted. trans_fat_g is confirmed 0.0 per HPB source — no hydrogenated fats are used in traditional Kuih Lapis. cholesterol_mg is confirmed 0.0 — Kuih Lapis contains no animal-derived ingredients (coconut milk is plant-based). vitamin_a_mcg_rae is 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB source; no carotenoid-rich ingredients). vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — no animal products). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — no animal products or fortification). GI value of 70 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database component data (tapioca starch GI ~70–95; rice flour GI ~70–80); no direct GI measurement for Kuih Lapis exists in the approved GI database. Hindi local name 'कुइह लापिस' is a transliteration; no established Hindi culinary term exists for this dish. FoodEx2 code A0CEK used as closest parent; no exact Kuih Lapis entry exists in FoodEx2.
